1:
"At the end of every seven years thou shalt make a release." The witnesses note the year of release every seventh year — a release of debts, not yet of servants. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was not of servants, who served six years from their own start, but the sabbatical release of debts. The witnesses note that it came at the end of every seven years. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the end of seven years — the sabbatical year; the thou shalt make a release — a remission of debts; the mercy to the poor — none ground down forever; the a type of jubilee — the gospel release of debt (Luke 4:19).

The witnesses agree the sabbatical "release" was a merciful institution woven into Israel's calendar: every seventh year debts were to be remitted, a periodic lifting of the burden from the poor; the regular return of the release shows God's care that none be ground down forever by debt, and pictures, in its kind, the greater release of the gospel — the year of jubilee and forgiveness in which the debt no man could pay is cancelled by grace (cf. Luke 4:19, the acceptable year of the LORD).

So the witnesses make the verse the year of release: The witnesses note the the end of seven years: the sabbatical year. The witnesses note the thou shalt make a release: a remission of debts. The witnesses note the mercy to the poor: none ground down forever. The witnesses note the a type of jubilee: the gospel release of debt (Luke 4:19). The expositors observe the the end of seven years: the sabbatical year. The expositors observe the thou shalt make a release: a remission of debts. The expositors observe the mercy to the poor: none ground down forever. The expositors observe the a type of jubilee: the gospel release of debt (Luke 4:19). The believer is taught to mark the the end of seven years, the thou shalt make a release, the mercy to the poor, the a type of jubilee, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that every seventh year debts were to be released — a merciful lifting of the burden, picturing the greater release of the gospel, the acceptable year of the LORD (Luke 4:19).
2:
"And this is the manner of the release: Every creditor that lendeth ought unto his neighbour shall release it... because it is called the LORD'S release." The witnesses note the rule — the creditor was to release the debt of his neighbour, for it is the LORD's release. The witnesses note that Gill notes this gives the rules to be observed in making the release. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set it — he shall not exact it of his neighbour or brother, because it is called the LORD's release. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the manner of the release — the rule given; the release thy neighbour — not exacting the debt; the the LORD's release — god's own appointment; the mercy as obedience — we forgive because forgiven (Matt. 18:33).

The witnesses agree the naming of it "the LORD's release" gives the act both its authority and its spirit: the creditor was not to exact the debt of his brother, because the release was God's own appointment and done for his sake; the phrase lifts the remission above mere human kindness to a sacred duty owed to the LORD, who claims the forgiveness of debts as a service to himself — teaching that mercy to the brother is reckoned as obedience to God, and that we forgive others' debts because our own great debt is forgiven (cf. Matt. 18:33).

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD's release: The witnesses note the the manner of the release: the rule given. The witnesses note the release thy neighbour: not exacting the debt. The witnesses note the the LORD's release: God's own appointment. The witnesses note the mercy as obedience: we forgive because forgiven (Matt. 18:33). The expositors observe the the manner of the release: the rule given. The expositors observe the release thy neighbour: not exacting the debt. The expositors observe the the LORD's release: God's own appointment. The expositors observe the mercy as obedience: we forgive because forgiven (Matt. 18:33). The believer is taught to mark the the manner of the release, the release thy neighbour, the the LORD's release, the mercy as obedience, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that the creditor was to release his neighbour's debt, for it is "the LORD's release" — mercy to the brother reckoned as obedience to God, forgiving others' debts because our own is forgiven (Matt. 18:33).
3:
"Of a foreigner thou mayest exact it again: but that which is thine with thy brother thine hand shall release;" The witnesses note the exception — the debt of a foreigner might still be exacted, but not a brother's. The witnesses note that Gill notes of a foreigner the debt might be exacted, on the seventh year or after. The witnesses note that Calvin notes it was just that despisers of the Law should not enjoy the sabbatical benefit, since God had appointed it for his own people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the foreigner — his debt might be exacted; the thy brother released — the covenant privilege; the the sabbatical benefit — for God's own people; the the bond of the brethren — special love within the household (Gal. 6:10).

The reformed reader (Calvin) explains the distinction: the release was a privilege of the covenant people, and it was not unjust that the foreigner — a stranger to the Law and its sabbath — should be held to his debt while the brother was freed; the difference marks the special bond of the covenant community, within which a peculiar mercy reigned, the brethren bound to forbear one another as those under the same gracious Law; the household of faith owes a love and forbearance to its own beyond what is owed to strangers (cf. Gal. 6:10).

So the witnesses make the verse brother and foreigner: The witnesses note the the foreigner: his debt might be exacted. The witnesses note the thy brother released: the covenant privilege. The witnesses note the the sabbatical benefit: for God's own people. The witnesses note the the bond of the brethren: special love within the household (Gal. 6:10). The expositors observe the the foreigner: his debt might be exacted. The expositors observe the thy brother released: the covenant privilege. The expositors observe the the sabbatical benefit: for God's own people. The expositors observe the the bond of the brethren: special love within the household (Gal. 6:10). The believer is taught to mark the the foreigner, the thy brother released, the the sabbatical benefit, the the bond of the brethren, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that the foreigner's debt might be exacted but the brother's released — the special mercy of the covenant community, the household of faith owing a peculiar love and forbearance to its own (Gal. 6:10).
4:
"Save when there shall be no poor among you; for the LORD shall greatly bless thee in the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee for an inheritance to possess it:" The witnesses note the ideal — that if obedient and blessed, there would be no poor needing release. The witnesses note that Gill notes the law was never meant to screen the able from paying just debts, but only the truly poor; were they obedient, there would be no debts to release. The witnesses note that the LORD would greatly bless them in the land. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the no poor among you — the ideal of obedience; the the LORD shall bless thee — his abounding favour; the want banished — poverty not God's lack; the a glimpse of plenty — none lacking among the faithful (Acts 4:34).

The witnesses agree the verse holds out an ideal bound to obedience: if Israel walked faithfully with God, his blessing would so abound that "there shall be no poor among you," none needing the release; the promise shows that poverty among God's people is not God's lack but often the fruit of disobedience, and that his blessing, fully enjoyed, would banish want — a glimpse of what a wholly obedient and trusting people might know, the abundance of God leaving none in need (cf. Acts 4:34, neither was there any among them that lacked).

So the witnesses make the verse no poor if blessed: The witnesses note the no poor among you: the ideal of obedience. The witnesses note the the LORD shall bless thee: his abounding favour. The witnesses note the want banished: poverty not God's lack. The witnesses note the a glimpse of plenty: none lacking among the faithful (Acts 4:34). The expositors observe the no poor among you: the ideal of obedience. The expositors observe the the LORD shall bless thee: his abounding favour. The expositors observe the want banished: poverty not God's lack. The expositors observe the a glimpse of plenty: none lacking among the faithful (Acts 4:34). The believer is taught to mark the no poor among you, the the LORD shall bless thee, the want banished, the a glimpse of plenty, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that if obedient and blessed there would be no poor among them — the ideal bound to obedience, God's abundance able to banish want, a glimpse of a people among whom none lacked (Acts 4:34).
5:
"Only if thou carefully hearken unto the voice of the LORD thy God, to observe to do all these commandments which I command thee this day." The witnesses note the condition of the blessing — careful obedience to all God's commandments. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, this being the case, there would be no more poor among them, or they would be so blessed as to release the poor's debts without hurting themselves. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the condition of careful hearkening. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the carefully hearken — the condition of blessing; the observe to do all — diligent obedience; the the plenty conditioned — blessing the fruit of faithfulness; the obedience the way — his favour flowing on it (Deut. 28:1).

The witnesses agree the promised plenty hung upon a careful obedience: "only if thou carefully hearken" — the blessing conditioned on a diligent keeping of all God's commandments; the word teaches that the abundance which would banish poverty was not unconditional but the fruit of faithfulness, God binding his temporal blessings on Israel to their obedience; the principle abides, that the way of obedience is the way of blessing, and that careful attention to God's voice is the path on which his favour flows (cf. Deut. 28:1).

So the witnesses make the verse if thou hearken: The witnesses note the carefully hearken: the condition of blessing. The witnesses note the observe to do all: diligent obedience. The witnesses note the the plenty conditioned: blessing the fruit of faithfulness. The witnesses note the obedience the way: his favour flowing on it (Deut. 28:1). The expositors observe the carefully hearken: the condition of blessing. The expositors observe the observe to do all: diligent obedience. The expositors observe the the plenty conditioned: blessing the fruit of faithfulness. The expositors observe the obedience the way: his favour flowing on it (Deut. 28:1). The believer is taught to mark the carefully hearken, the observe to do all, the the plenty conditioned, the obedience the way, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that the blessing hung on careful obedience to all God's commandments — the plenty the fruit of faithfulness, the way of obedience the way of blessing (Deut. 28:1).
6:
"For the LORD thy God blesseth thee, as he promised thee: and thou shalt lend unto many nations, but thou shalt not borrow..." The witnesses note the promised prosperity — Israel lending to many nations and reigning over them, not borrowing or being ruled. The witnesses note that Gill notes God is faithful who promised, and gives the blessing according to the nature of the promise — conditional temporal promises given as the condition is performed. The witnesses note that they would lend to many nations and not borrow. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD blesseth thee — the faithful promise kept; the lend unto many nations — the place of plenty; the not borrow — never reduced to subjection; the rich to give — made a blessing to others (2 Cor. 9:11).

The witnesses agree the blessing rises to a place of honour and plenty among the nations: a faithful Israel would be so prospered as to "lend unto many nations" and reign over them, never reduced to borrowing or subjection; the promise, conditional on obedience, marks the dignity God sets on a people that walks with him — head and not tail, lender and not debtor; and it points beyond temporal wealth to the spiritual abundance of the people of God, made rich in him to be a blessing to others, that they may have to give and not to ask (cf. 2 Cor. 9:11).

So the witnesses make the verse lend, not borrow: The witnesses note the the LORD blesseth thee: the faithful promise kept. The witnesses note the lend unto many nations: the place of plenty. The witnesses note the not borrow: never reduced to subjection. The witnesses note the rich to give: made a blessing to others (2 Cor. 9:11). The expositors observe the the LORD blesseth thee: the faithful promise kept. The expositors observe the lend unto many nations: the place of plenty. The expositors observe the not borrow: never reduced to subjection. The expositors observe the rich to give: made a blessing to others (2 Cor. 9:11). The believer is taught to mark the the LORD blesseth thee, the lend unto many nations, the not borrow, the rich to give, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that a blessed Israel would lend to many nations and not borrow — the dignity God sets on a people that walks with him, made rich to give and to bless others (2 Cor. 9:11).
7:
"If there be among you a poor man of one of thy brethren within any of thy gates... thou shalt not harden thine heart, nor shut thine hand from thy poor brother:" The witnesses note the charge, where there is a poor brother, not to harden the heart nor shut the hand. The witnesses note that Gill notes there would be poor among them if they did not keep the commandments, as the Targums say. The witnesses note that Ellicott notes the poor of one's own city come first, "the poor of thine own city before the poor of another." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a poor brother — within thy gates; the harden not thine heart — the inward disposition; the shut not thine hand — the outward act follows; the charity begins near — the poor of thine own city first (1 John 3:17).

The witnesses agree the duty to the poor is pressed both negatively and in its proper order: the heart was "not to be hardened" nor the hand shut against a poor brother, and charity begins near, the poor of one's own gates having the first claim; the doubled warning against a hard heart and a closed hand shows that true charity is first an inward disposition — for the shut hand betrays the hardened heart — so that the relief of the poor begins in a heart moved with compassion, the opposite of the cold selfishness that turns away (cf. 1 John 3:17).

So the witnesses make the verse harden not the heart: The witnesses note the a poor brother: within thy gates. The witnesses note the harden not thine heart: the inward disposition. The witnesses note the shut not thine hand: the outward act follows. The witnesses note the charity begins near: the poor of thine own city first (1 John 3:17). The expositors observe the a poor brother: within thy gates. The expositors observe the harden not thine heart: the inward disposition. The expositors observe the shut not thine hand: the outward act follows. The expositors observe the charity begins near: the poor of thine own city first (1 John 3:17). The believer is taught to mark the a poor brother, the harden not thine heart, the shut not thine hand, the charity begins near, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that they were not to harden the heart nor shut the hand to a poor brother — true charity an inward compassion before an outward act, the shut hand betraying the hardened heart (1 John 3:17).
8:
"But thou shalt open thine hand wide unto him, and shalt surely lend him sufficient for his need, in that which he wanteth." The witnesses note the positive charge — to open the hand wide and supply the poor brother sufficient for his need. The witnesses note that Gill notes the hand is to be opened wide and given bountifully, the heart first opened, the affections moved, a willing mind disposed to give generously. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the lending of sufficient for his need. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the open thine hand wide — a free liberality; the lend him sufficient — enough for his need; the the heart opened first — the affections moved; the full, free giving — proportioned to necessity (2 Cor. 9:7).

The witnesses agree the charge calls for a free and ample liberality: the hand "opened wide," giving "sufficient for his need" — not a grudging trifle but a generous supply measured to the want; Gill marks that the open hand follows an opened heart, the affections first moved to a willing generosity; the law thus teaches not bare almsgiving but a large-hearted bounty that meets the real need of the poor, the model of a giving that is full, free, and proportioned to necessity (cf. 2 Cor. 9:7).

So the witnesses make the verse open thine hand wide: The witnesses note the open thine hand wide: a free liberality. The witnesses note the lend him sufficient: enough for his need. The witnesses note the the heart opened first: the affections moved. The witnesses note the full, free giving: proportioned to necessity (2 Cor. 9:7). The expositors observe the open thine hand wide: a free liberality. The expositors observe the lend him sufficient: enough for his need. The expositors observe the the heart opened first: the affections moved. The expositors observe the full, free giving: proportioned to necessity (2 Cor. 9:7). The believer is taught to mark the open thine hand wide, the lend him sufficient, the the heart opened first, the full, free giving, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that they were to open the hand wide and supply the poor brother's need — a free, ample liberality flowing from an opened heart, giving full and proportioned to necessity (2 Cor. 9:7).
9:
"Beware that there be not a thought in thy wicked heart, saying, The seventh year, the year of release, is at hand; and thine eye be evil against thy poor brother, and thou givest him nought;" The witnesses note the warning against the selfish thought that withholds, fearing the release would cancel the loan. The witnesses note that Gill notes the "heart of Belial" — the worthless heart without the yoke of God's law, in which Satan works to incline to evil thoughts. The witnesses note that the evil thought was to refuse the poor as the year of release drew near. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a thought in thy wicked heart — the covetous excuse; the the year of release is at hand — fearing the loan cancelled; the thine eye be evil — grudging the poor; the love gives without gain — lend hoping for nothing again (Luke 6:35).

The witnesses agree the verse lays bare the subtle excuse of a covetous heart: as the year of release approached, when the loan would be cancelled, the grudging mind would refuse to lend, counting it loss; God names this a "wicked heart," a heart of Belial unyoked to his law, in which selfishness reasons against mercy; the warning exposes how covetousness dresses itself in prudence, and how the law of love demands giving even where no return can be hoped — the very test of charity being to give where gain is impossible (cf. Luke 6:35, lend, hoping for nothing again).

So the witnesses make the verse the wicked thought: The witnesses note the a thought in thy wicked heart: the covetous excuse. The witnesses note the the year of release is at hand: fearing the loan cancelled. The witnesses note the thine eye be evil: grudging the poor. The witnesses note the love gives without gain: lend hoping for nothing again (Luke 6:35). The expositors observe the a thought in thy wicked heart: the covetous excuse. The expositors observe the the year of release is at hand: fearing the loan cancelled. The expositors observe the thine eye be evil: grudging the poor. The expositors observe the love gives without gain: lend hoping for nothing again (Luke 6:35). The believer is taught to mark the a thought in thy wicked heart, the the year of release is at hand, the thine eye be evil, the love gives without gain, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that they were to beware the wicked thought that withholds as the release nears — covetousness dressed as prudence, the law of love demanding to give where no return is hoped (Luke 6:35).
10:
"Thou shalt surely give him, and thine heart shall not be grieved when thou givest unto him: because that for this thing the LORD thy God shall bless thee in all thy works..." The witnesses note the charge to give freely and ungrudgingly, with the promise that God would bless the giver. The witnesses note that Gill notes he must surely give, even a hundred times if asked, to the limit of what is sufficient for present want. The witnesses note that the heart was not to be grieved, and God would bless him for it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the surely give him — freely and fully; the thine heart not grieved — without grudging; the the LORD shall bless thee — the giver rewarded; the cheerful giving — god loveth a cheerful giver (2 Cor. 9:7).

The witnesses agree the manner of the gift matters as much as the gift: it must be given "and thine heart shall not be grieved" — freely, without grudging or regret — and the promise is added that "for this thing the LORD shall bless thee"; the cheerfulness of the giver is required because God loves it and rewards it, the open and glad hand the channel of his blessing back upon the giver; the law thus joins liberality and joy, and pledges that giving is no loss but the way of gain, "the LORD shall bless thee in all thy works" (cf. 2 Cor. 9:7, God loveth a cheerful giver).

So the witnesses make the verse give without grieving: The witnesses note the surely give him: freely and fully. The witnesses note the thine heart not grieved: without grudging. The witnesses note the the LORD shall bless thee: the giver rewarded. The witnesses note the cheerful giving: God loveth a cheerful giver (2 Cor. 9:7). The expositors observe the surely give him: freely and fully. The expositors observe the thine heart not grieved: without grudging. The expositors observe the the LORD shall bless thee: the giver rewarded. The expositors observe the cheerful giving: God loveth a cheerful giver (2 Cor. 9:7). The believer is taught to mark the surely give him, the thine heart not grieved, the the LORD shall bless thee, the cheerful giving, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that they were to give freely without a grieved heart, for God would bless them — the cheerful giver loved and rewarded, liberality no loss but the way of gain (2 Cor. 9:7).
11:
"For the poor shall never cease out of the land: therefore I command thee, saying, Thou shalt open thine hand wide unto thy brother, to thy poor, and to thy needy, in thy land." The witnesses note that the poor will always be in the land, so the hand is ever to be open to them. The witnesses note that Gill notes there would always be such objects for charity, no contradiction to the earlier word — for had they been obedient, there would have been none, but God foresaw they would not keep his commands. The witnesses note that therefore the hand was to be opened wide to the poor and needy. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the poor shall never cease — ever present in the land; the therefore I command thee — the standing duty; the open thine hand wide — perpetual charity; the the poor always with you — an abiding call to mercy (Matt. 26:11).

The witnesses agree the perpetual presence of the poor is made the ground of perpetual charity: because "the poor shall never cease out of the land," the open hand is a standing duty, never to be withdrawn; Gill reconciles this with the earlier ideal — there would be no poor if Israel were obedient, but God foresaw they would not be, so the poor remain as a continual call to mercy; our Lord took up this very word, "the poor always ye have with you" (Matt. 26:11), the abiding poor an abiding occasion for the love that opens its hand.

So the witnesses make the verse the poor never cease: The witnesses note the the poor shall never cease: ever present in the land. The witnesses note the therefore I command thee: the standing duty. The witnesses note the open thine hand wide: perpetual charity. The witnesses note the the poor always with you: an abiding call to mercy (Matt. 26:11). The expositors observe the the poor shall never cease: ever present in the land. The expositors observe the therefore I command thee: the standing duty. The expositors observe the open thine hand wide: perpetual charity. The expositors observe the the poor always with you: an abiding call to mercy (Matt. 26:11). The believer is taught to mark the the poor shall never cease, the therefore I command thee, the open thine hand wide, the the poor always with you, and to open his hand wide to the poor. The verse thus records that the poor would never cease, so the hand was ever to be open — the abiding poor an abiding occasion for charity, the poor always with us a standing call to the love that gives (Matt. 26:11).
12:
"And if thy brother, an Hebrew man, or an Hebrew woman, be sold unto thee, and serve thee six years; then in the seventh year thou shalt let him go free from thee." The witnesses note the law freeing the Hebrew servant in the seventh year of his service. The witnesses note that Gill notes a man might be sold by the sanhedrim for theft, or a father might sell his daughter under age, or one might sell himself for poverty; this law refers to such cases. The witnesses note that after six years' service he was to go free in the seventh. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an Hebrew sold — by various causes; the serve thee six years — the term of service; the let him go free — in the seventh year; the a bound to bondage — no endless slavery among brethren.

The witnesses agree the law set a merciful bound to bondage among the brethren: a Hebrew sold into service, by whatever cause, was not to be held forever but freed in the seventh year of his service; the limit shows God's care that no Israelite be ground down in perpetual slavery to a brother, the bond of common redemption forbidding a harsh and endless servitude; the freeing of the servant in the seventh year is a small sabbath of liberty, picturing the release God gives his own from bondage, that the brethren deal with one another as a free and redeemed people.

So the witnesses make the verse freed in the seventh year: The witnesses note the an Hebrew sold: by various causes. The witnesses note the serve thee six years: the term of service. The witnesses note the let him go free: in the seventh year. The witnesses note the a bound to bondage: no endless slavery among brethren. The expositors observe the an Hebrew sold: by various causes. The expositors observe the serve thee six years: the term of service. The expositors observe the let him go free: in the seventh year. The expositors observe the a bound to bondage: no endless slavery among brethren. The believer is taught to mark the an Hebrew sold, the serve thee six years, the let him go free, the a bound to bondage, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the Hebrew servant was freed in the seventh year — a merciful bound to bondage, the brethren dealing as a free and redeemed people, a small sabbath of liberty.
13:
"And when thou sendest him out free from thee, thou shalt not let him go away empty:" The witnesses note that the freed servant was not to be sent away empty-handed. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was when he discharged him from servitude and made him a free man. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the charge — thou shalt not let him go away empty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the sendest him out free — the release granted; the not go away empty — furnished, not abandoned; the a provision given — for a new start; the kindness crowning freedom — equipped to use his liberty.

The witnesses agree the law would not let liberty be barren: the servant set free was "not to go away empty," but to be sent off with a provision; the charge guards against a grudging release that gives freedom but nothing to live on, requiring the master to send his servant out furnished for a new start; the generosity teaches that true kindness does not merely end an obligation but provides for the one released, the freed man not abandoned to want but equipped — a liberality that crowns the gift of freedom with the means to use it.

So the witnesses make the verse not empty: The witnesses note the sendest him out free: the release granted. The witnesses note the not go away empty: furnished, not abandoned. The witnesses note the a provision given: for a new start. The witnesses note the kindness crowning freedom: equipped to use his liberty. The expositors observe the sendest him out free: the release granted. The expositors observe the not go away empty: furnished, not abandoned. The expositors observe the a provision given: for a new start. The expositors observe the kindness crowning freedom: equipped to use his liberty. The believer is taught to mark the sendest him out free, the not go away empty, the a provision given, the kindness crowning freedom, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the freed servant was not to go away empty — liberty not left barren, the master to send him out furnished, kindness crowning the gift of freedom with the means to use it.
14:
"Thou shalt furnish him liberally out of thy flock, and out of thy floor, and out of thy winepress: of that wherewith the LORD thy God hath blessed thee thou shalt give unto him." The witnesses note the charge to furnish the freed servant liberally from flock, floor, and winepress. The witnesses note that Gill notes "loading thou shalt load him" — give as much as he can carry, to supply his present want and set him up in the world. The witnesses note that he was to give of that wherewith the LORD had blessed him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the furnish him liberally — load him with provision; the flock, floor, winepress — grain, wine, and beasts; the of God's blessing — what the master freely received; the freely give — gratitude turned to generosity (Matt. 10:8).

The witnesses agree the parting gift was to be lavish, not token: the master was to "furnish him liberally" — to load him with flock, grain, and wine, enough to set him up afresh — giving "of that wherewith the LORD hath blessed thee"; the giving is grounded in the giver's own blessedness, that what he had freely received he should freely impart; the law thus turns the master's gratitude to God into generosity to man, the freed servant sent away not bare but enriched, that he might begin his liberty with a competence drawn from his master's plenty (cf. Matt. 10:8, freely ye have received, freely give).

So the witnesses make the verse furnish liberally: The witnesses note the furnish him liberally: load him with provision. The witnesses note the flock, floor, winepress: grain, wine, and beasts. The witnesses note the of God's blessing: what the master freely received. The witnesses note the freely give: gratitude turned to generosity (Matt. 10:8). The expositors observe the furnish him liberally: load him with provision. The expositors observe the flock, floor, winepress: grain, wine, and beasts. The expositors observe the of God's blessing: what the master freely received. The expositors observe the freely give: gratitude turned to generosity (Matt. 10:8). The believer is taught to mark the furnish him liberally, the flock, floor, winepress, the of God's blessing, the freely give, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the master was to furnish the freed servant liberally from his own blessing — what he freely received he should freely give, the servant sent away enriched, not bare (Matt. 10:8).
15:
"And thou shalt remember that thou wast a bondman in the land of Egypt, and the LORD thy God redeemed thee: therefore I command thee this thing to day." The witnesses note the ground of the kindness — to remember their own bondage in Egypt and God's redemption. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were used hardly in Egypt, their lives made bitter, and so should show the greater compassion to servants, sympathizing with their case. The witnesses note that Ellicott sets the same — remember thou wast a bondman. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the thou wast a bondman — in the land of Egypt; the the LORD redeemed thee — their own deliverance; the therefore deal kindly — compassion from fellow-feeling; the the redeemed redeeming — redemption shaping their giving (Eph. 4:32).

The witnesses agree the master's generosity is rooted in his own remembered redemption: because Israel had been bondmen in Egypt and the LORD had redeemed them, they were to deal kindly and bountifully with their servants, doing as they had been done by; the memory of their own deliverance was to soften them toward the bound, and the pattern of God's free redemption to shape their giving — that those who have been freed by grace should free and enrich others, the redeemed showing redemption's kindness, "freely ye have received" made the rule of their dealing (cf. Eph. 4:32).

So the witnesses make the verse remember thy bondage: The witnesses note the thou wast a bondman: in the land of Egypt. The witnesses note the the LORD redeemed thee: their own deliverance. The witnesses note the therefore deal kindly: compassion from fellow-feeling. The witnesses note the the redeemed redeeming: redemption shaping their giving (Eph. 4:32). The expositors observe the thou wast a bondman: in the land of Egypt. The expositors observe the the LORD redeemed thee: their own deliverance. The expositors observe the therefore deal kindly: compassion from fellow-feeling. The expositors observe the the redeemed redeeming: redemption shaping their giving (Eph. 4:32). The believer is taught to mark the thou wast a bondman, the the LORD redeemed thee, the therefore deal kindly, the the redeemed redeeming, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that they were to remember their bondage and redemption, and so deal kindly with their servants — the redeemed showing redemption's kindness, those freed by grace freeing and enriching others (Eph. 4:32).
16:
"And it shall be, if he say unto thee, I will not go away from thee; because he loveth thee and thine house, because he is well with thee;" The witnesses note the case of the servant who, loving his master, chooses to stay rather than go free. The witnesses note that Gill notes the servant might say he would not go away, nor quit his service. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the reason — because he loveth thee and thy house, and is well with thee. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I will not go away — choosing to stay; the because he loveth thee — love, not need; the he is well with thee — the master's service better than freedom; the a figure of Christ — the willing servant who delights to serve (Ps. 40:7-8).

The witnesses agree the willing servant's choice springs from love, not need: he stays "because he loveth thee and thine house," finding his master's service better than his own freedom; the case reveals a bond deeper than law — a love that prefers glad service to bare liberty, choosing to belong; herein the fathers have seen a figure of Christ, the willing servant who, loving the Father and his house, said "lo, I come... I delight to do thy will" (Ps. 40:7-8), and of the believer who counts the service of God a freedom dearer than the world's liberty.

So the witnesses make the verse the willing servant: The witnesses note the I will not go away: choosing to stay. The witnesses note the because he loveth thee: love, not need. The witnesses note the he is well with thee: the master's service better than freedom. The witnesses note the a figure of Christ: the willing servant who delights to serve (Ps. 40:7-8). The expositors observe the I will not go away: choosing to stay. The expositors observe the because he loveth thee: love, not need. The expositors observe the he is well with thee: the master's service better than freedom. The expositors observe the a figure of Christ: the willing servant who delights to serve (Ps. 40:7-8). The believer is taught to mark the I will not go away, the because he loveth thee, the he is well with thee, the a figure of Christ, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the servant might stay out of love for his master — glad service preferred to bare liberty, a figure of Christ the willing servant who delighted to do the Father's will (Ps. 40:7-8).
17:
"Then thou shalt take an awl, and thrust it through his ear unto the door, and he shall be thy servant for ever..." The witnesses note the rite of the bored ear, marking the servant who chose to serve forever. The witnesses note that Gill notes the awl was thrust through his ear unto the door — not of the master's house but, by the Targum, of the court of judicature, before whom he declared his desire to continue. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the boring of the ear, the servant his for ever. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the an awl through his ear — the solemn rite; the unto the door — before the judges; the thy servant for ever — perpetual, chosen service; the the opened ear — a type of Christ and the willing bondservant (Ps. 40:6).

The witnesses agree the bored ear was the solemn token of a freely chosen, perpetual service: brought before the judges, the willing servant had his ear pierced to the door, sealing his choice to belong to his master "for ever"; the ear, the organ of hearing and obedience, was fittingly marked, the servant binding himself to a lifelong listening and serving; the fathers see here a type of Christ, of whom it is written "mine ears hast thou opened" (Ps. 40:6), and of every believer who, by love, makes himself the willing bondservant of God, his ear opened to obey.

So the witnesses make the verse the bored ear: The witnesses note the an awl through his ear: the solemn rite. The witnesses note the unto the door: before the judges. The witnesses note the thy servant for ever: perpetual, chosen service. The witnesses note the the opened ear: a type of Christ and the willing bondservant (Ps. 40:6). The expositors observe the an awl through his ear: the solemn rite. The expositors observe the unto the door: before the judges. The expositors observe the thy servant for ever: perpetual, chosen service. The expositors observe the the opened ear: a type of Christ and the willing bondservant (Ps. 40:6). The believer is taught to mark the an awl through his ear, the unto the door, the thy servant for ever, the the opened ear, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the willing servant's ear was bored to the door, his master's for ever — the token of a freely chosen, lifelong service, a type of Christ whose ear was opened to obey (Ps. 40:6).
18:
"It shall not seem hard unto thee, when thou sendest him away free from thee; for he hath been worth a double hired servant to thee, in serving thee six years..." The witnesses note the charge not to grudge the servant his freedom and parting gift, for his service had been doubly worth the cost. The witnesses note that Gill notes the master should not grudge him his liberty nor what is given at his dismissal. The witnesses note that Calvin notes how difficult this law was to the niggardly Jews, whom God reproves for their mean and grudging spirit. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not seem hard — grudge not his freedom; the worth a double servant — his service earned much; the the master loses nothing — generosity no loss; the the niggardly rebuked — releasing without regret.

The reformed reader (Calvin) marks the rebuke hidden in the verse: God foresaw that the grudging would find it "hard" to free and furnish their servants, and so reasons with their meanness — the servant had earned his keep twice over, being "worth a double hired servant," so the master lost nothing by his generosity; the argument shames a niggardly spirit and presses a cheerful liberality, that what is given to a faithful servant is no loss but a debt of gratitude, and the open hand is to release without regret what justice and kindness alike require.

So the witnesses make the verse not seem hard: The witnesses note the not seem hard: grudge not his freedom. The witnesses note the worth a double servant: his service earned much. The witnesses note the the master loses nothing: generosity no loss. The witnesses note the the niggardly rebuked: releasing without regret. The expositors observe the not seem hard: grudge not his freedom. The expositors observe the worth a double servant: his service earned much. The expositors observe the the master loses nothing: generosity no loss. The expositors observe the the niggardly rebuked: releasing without regret. The believer is taught to mark the not seem hard, the worth a double servant, the the master loses nothing, the the niggardly rebuked, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the master was not to grudge the servant his freedom, for he had been worth a double hired servant — meanness rebuked, generosity no loss, the open hand releasing without regret what kindness requires.
19:
"All the firstling males that come of thy herd and of thy flock thou shalt sanctify unto the LORD thy God: thou shalt do no work with the firstling of thy bullock, nor shear the firstling of thy sheep." The witnesses note the firstling males set apart wholly to the LORD, not to be worked or shorn. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was according to the law of the firstborn already given. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the firstlings sanctified, with no work or shearing. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the firstling males — of herd and flock; the sanctify unto the LORD — wholly God's; the no work nor shearing — no private gain taken; the the first and best his — the firstborn pointing to Christ (Col. 1:15).

The witnesses agree the firstling belonged wholly to God and so to no common use: sanctified to the LORD, it was not to be worked nor its wool shorn, the owner taking no profit of his own from what was God's; the rule teaches that what is devoted to God is to be kept sacred and undiminished, not turned to private gain, the firstborn of the flock a continual acknowledgment that the first and best is the LORD's — and the firstborn throughout pointing to Christ, "the firstborn of every creature" (Col. 1:15), wholly consecrated to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the firstling sanctified: The witnesses note the the firstling males: of herd and flock. The witnesses note the sanctify unto the LORD: wholly God's. The witnesses note the no work nor shearing: no private gain taken. The witnesses note the the first and best his: the firstborn pointing to Christ (Col. 1:15). The expositors observe the the firstling males: of herd and flock. The expositors observe the sanctify unto the LORD: wholly God's. The expositors observe the no work nor shearing: no private gain taken. The expositors observe the the first and best his: the firstborn pointing to Christ (Col. 1:15). The believer is taught to mark the the firstling males, the sanctify unto the LORD, the no work nor shearing, the the first and best his, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the firstling was sanctified wholly to the LORD, not worked or shorn — what is devoted to God kept sacred and undiminished, the firstborn the LORD's, pointing to Christ the firstborn (Col. 1:15).
20:
"Thou shalt eat it before the LORD thy God year by year in the place which the LORD shall choose, thou and thy household." The witnesses note the firstling eaten before the LORD at the chosen place, year by year. The witnesses note that Gill notes that if of male firstlings, only the priests might eat, being devoted to the Lord; if of the people in common, then of female or second firstlings. The witnesses note that it was eaten before the LORD, in the chosen place, with the household. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eat it before the LORD — a sacred meal; the year by year — a yearly communion; the in the chosen place — at the place of his name; the thou and thy household — the family in thankful fellowship.

The witnesses agree the eating of the firstling before the LORD made it a sacred, shared meal: the devoted animal was eaten "before the LORD" at the place of his name, year by year, by priest or household as the case required; the holy feast joined the worshipper's family in a yearly act of thankful communion over what God had claimed as his own, the first of the flock becoming the matter of a meal in his presence — religion again clothed in gladness, the devoted gift returned, in part, as a feast of fellowship before the Giver.

So the witnesses make the verse eaten before the LORD: The witnesses note the eat it before the LORD: a sacred meal. The witnesses note the year by year: a yearly communion. The witnesses note the in the chosen place: at the place of his name. The witnesses note the thou and thy household: the family in thankful fellowship. The expositors observe the eat it before the LORD: a sacred meal. The expositors observe the year by year: a yearly communion. The expositors observe the in the chosen place: at the place of his name. The expositors observe the thou and thy household: the family in thankful fellowship. The believer is taught to mark the eat it before the LORD, the year by year, the in the chosen place, the thou and thy household, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the firstling was eaten before the LORD year by year with the household — the devoted gift made a feast of thankful fellowship before the Giver, religion clothed in gladness.
21:
"And if there be any blemish therein, as if it be lame, or blind, or have any ill blemish, thou shalt not sacrifice it unto the LORD thy God." The witnesses note that a blemished firstling was not to be offered in sacrifice to the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes if there be any blemish, as lame or blind. The witnesses note that Clarke notes God will have both a perfect priest and a perfect offering (Lev. 22:20). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the if there be any blemish — lame or blind; the thou shalt not sacrifice it — unfit for the altar; the a perfect offering — god accepting nothing defective; the the unblemished Lamb — christ without spot (1 Pet. 1:19).

The witnesses agree the rule guards the holiness and perfection due to God in his offerings: a firstling lame, blind, or otherwise blemished was unfit for sacrifice, for God would have nothing maimed or defective on his altar; Clarke draws the abiding principle that "God will have both a perfect priest and a perfect offering" — the demand for an unblemished victim pointing to the one perfect sacrifice, Christ "a lamb without blemish and without spot" (1 Pet. 1:19), and rebuking every offering to God of what is faulty, careless, or second-best.

So the witnesses make the verse no blemished offering: The witnesses note the if there be any blemish: lame or blind. The witnesses note the thou shalt not sacrifice it: unfit for the altar. The witnesses note the a perfect offering: God accepting nothing defective. The witnesses note the the unblemished Lamb: Christ without spot (1 Pet. 1:19). The expositors observe the if there be any blemish: lame or blind. The expositors observe the thou shalt not sacrifice it: unfit for the altar. The expositors observe the a perfect offering: God accepting nothing defective. The expositors observe the the unblemished Lamb: Christ without spot (1 Pet. 1:19). The believer is taught to mark the if there be any blemish, the thou shalt not sacrifice it, the a perfect offering, the the unblemished Lamb, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that a blemished firstling was not to be sacrificed — God requiring a perfect offering, the unblemished victim pointing to Christ the Lamb without spot, rebuking every faulty gift to God (1 Pet. 1:19).
22:
"Thou shalt eat it within thy gates: the unclean and the clean person shall eat it alike, as the roebuck, and as the hart." The witnesses note the blemished firstling eaten as common food at home, by clean and unclean alike. The witnesses note that Gill notes that, though it might not be sacrificed nor eaten as a sacred feast at Jerusalem, it might be eaten as common food in their own houses. The witnesses note that clean and unclean alike might eat it, as the roebuck and the hart. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the eat it within thy gates — as common meat at home; the clean and unclean alike — no sacred restriction; the as the roebuck and hart — like ordinary game; the nothing wasted — the imperfect put to lawful use.

The witnesses agree the blemished firstling, unfit for the altar, was not wasted but used as common meat: eaten at home, by clean and unclean alike, like ordinary game; the rule shows God's wise economy — what could not be offered to him was still given for man's use, the defective not destroyed but turned to a lawful end; the distinction kept between the sacred and the common teaches that what is holy must be without blemish, yet God in his goodness lets the imperfect serve the ordinary needs of life, nothing of his gifts needlessly lost.

So the witnesses make the verse eaten as common food: The witnesses note the eat it within thy gates: as common meat at home. The witnesses note the clean and unclean alike: no sacred restriction. The witnesses note the as the roebuck and hart: like ordinary game. The witnesses note the nothing wasted: the imperfect put to lawful use. The expositors observe the eat it within thy gates: as common meat at home. The expositors observe the clean and unclean alike: no sacred restriction. The expositors observe the as the roebuck and hart: like ordinary game. The expositors observe the nothing wasted: the imperfect put to lawful use. The believer is taught to mark the eat it within thy gates, the clean and unclean alike, the as the roebuck and hart, the nothing wasted, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the blemished firstling was eaten as common food by clean and unclean alike — what could not be offered still given for man's use, the sacred kept unblemished yet nothing of God's gifts needlessly lost.
23:
"Only thou shalt not eat the blood thereof; thou shalt pour it upon the ground as water." The witnesses note the one restriction even on the common-eaten firstling — the blood not to be eaten, but poured out. The witnesses note that Gill notes the blood was not to be eaten but poured upon the ground as water, as taught before (Deut. 12:16). The witnesses note that this held even for the firstling eaten as common food. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the not eat the blood — even of the common meat; the pour it on the ground — as water; the the blood is the life — sacred to God; the reserved for atonement — pointing to the blood of Christ (Heb. 9:22).

The witnesses agree the abiding ban on blood held even here: however the flesh might be eaten, the blood was never to be consumed but "poured upon the ground as water"; the law of the blood ran through all their eating, for the blood was the life, sacred to God and reserved for atonement; the constant pouring-out of the blood taught reverence for life and pointed to the blood of Christ, by which alone is atonement, "without shedding of blood is no remission" (Heb. 9:22) — the one thing never common, the blood held holy in every meal.

So the witnesses make the verse not the blood: The witnesses note the not eat the blood: even of the common meat. The witnesses note the pour it on the ground: as water. The witnesses note the the blood is the life: sacred to God. The witnesses note the reserved for atonement: pointing to the blood of Christ (Heb. 9:22). The expositors observe the not eat the blood: even of the common meat. The expositors observe the pour it on the ground: as water. The expositors observe the the blood is the life: sacred to God. The expositors observe the reserved for atonement: pointing to the blood of Christ (Heb. 9:22). The believer is taught to mark the not eat the blood, the pour it on the ground, the the blood is the life, the reserved for atonement, and to serve the LORD who redeemed him. The verse thus records that the blood of the firstling was never to be eaten but poured out — the abiding ban on blood, the life held sacred to God, pointing to the blood of Christ by which alone is atonement (Heb. 9:22).
